problem.passage:
5. Read the passage in <A> and the examples in <B>, and follow
the directions.【4 points】
<A>
Coordination by and, but, and orcan be divided into clausal
and phrasal coordination types, as demonstrated in (1a) and
(1b), respectively.
(1) a. [Jim speaks Spanish poorly] but [he got good scores on
the Spanish exam].
b. Jim speaks [French or German] poorly.
In many cases, a phrasal coordination can be expanded into
a logically equivalent clausal one. Compare sentence (2a)
with (2b). If (2a) is true, then (2b) is true, and vice versa. The
two sentences are logically equivalent and they can also be
said to share the same truth conditions.
introduced me to her [mother and father]. (2) a. Alice
i i
b. [Alice introduced me to her mother] and [she introduced
i i i
me to her father].
i
There are, however, some pairs of coordination sentences
that do not share the same truth conditions. Consider the
examples in (3), for instance. Under the condition where Sue
had coffee but not tea, (3b) is true but (3a) is false. Sentence
(3a) is true when it denotes that Sue had neither coffee nor tea.
(3) a. Sue didn’t have any [coffee or tea].
b. [Sue didn’t have any coffee] or [she didn’t have any
i i
tea].
Note: The subscript ‘’ indicates coreferentiality.
i
<B>
(4) a.Your car keys are [in your purse or on the dresser].
b.The [last and most telling] objection concerned the cost.
c.Fred [noticed but didn’t mention] Bill’s inconsistencies.
d.[Two perfect and four slightly damaged] copies were
found.
Based on the description in <A>, first, identify the ONE phrasal
coordination in the sentences in <B> that shows different truth
conditions from the clausal coordination, and then change it into
an appropriate clausal coordination. Second, explain how the
phrasal coordination you chose and the clausal coordination you
created differ in their truth conditions.
4
(8면 중 면)
